request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"friendster::social_communication","name":"Friendster","one_liner":"Pioneer social networking platform that grew to 115M users but was displaced by Facebook and ultimately pivoted to gaming before shutting down in 2015.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both are ad-supported B2C social platforms targeting consumers with free access monetized through ad revenue. However, the new pitch is a mobile-first, voice-note-centric app whereas Friendster was a broad web-based social graph platform. The monetization model (ad_revenue) is the same, but the product surface and content format differ meaningfully."},"market":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both target the consumer social networking market with a strong college/youth demographic angle. Friendster initially exploded on US college campuses and among young adults before pivoting to Asia. The new pitch also targets college populations. The core market thesis — viral social adoption among young adults — is identical."},"gtm":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both rely on viral, peer-driven organic growth within dense social clusters (college campuses). Friendster's early growth was word-of-mouth within friend circles; the new pitch explicitly calls out 'viral in college towers,' mirroring that same organic community GTM. Neither pitch describes paid acquisition as a primary channel."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Friendster was a well-funded, VC-backed platform (Kleiner Perkins, Benchmark) with over 115M registered users at peak. The new pitch appears to be at an early viral/traction stage with no mention of institutional funding. Friendster's scale dwarfs what is described in the pitch, making stage alignment moderate at best."}},"why_similar":"Both are ad-supported B2C consumer social platforms targeting young adults and relying on organic viral growth within tight geographic or social clusters (college campuses). Both monetize through ad revenue with no paywall. The underlying GTM bet — capture a dense community first, then expand — is structurally identical to Friendster's early playbook. The consumer social sector, customer type (consumer), and monetization model (ad_revenue) all match directly.","where_diverged":"1. Content format: The new pitch is built around 30-second voice notes, a highly constrained and novel content type, versus Friendster's general-purpose text, photos, video, and social graph features. 2. Location constraint: The new pitch anchors posts to a single recurring physical location per user, introducing a hyperlocal presence layer Friendster never had. 3. Mobile-first vs. web-first: The new pitch is implicitly a mobile app; Friendster was primarily a web platform for most of its life. 4. Geographic ambition: Friendster scaled globally and eventually concentrated in Southeast Asia; the new pitch is currently scoped to college towers with no stated global expansion plan. 5. Stage and funding: Friendster had $12M+ in institutional VC from launch; the new pitch shows no comparable backing.","failure_causes":["displacement by a better-executed competitor (Facebook)","poor technical performance and slow page loads alienating early adopters","repeated CEO turnover undermining strategic consistency","failure to monetize effectively before losing US market share","strategic blunder of declining Google's $30M acquisition offer in 2003","late pivot to social gaming that failed to retain the core user base","over-reliance on a shrinking Asian market without a credible re-entry into Western markets"],"lessons_for_input":["Lock in your core community's habits before a better-resourced competitor can clone your value proposition — Friendster lost the US market to Facebook in under two years after Facebook launched.","Prioritize technical scalability from day one; slow load times were a primary driver of Friendster's early user churn and directly enabled Facebook's takeover.","Do not delay monetization experimentation — Friendster raised $60M+ and still could not build a sustainable ad revenue engine before losing relevance.","Maintain leadership stability; Friendster cycled through five CEOs between 2004 and 2008, each resetting strategy and destroying execution continuity.","Define a defensible moat beyond the social graph — Friendster's patents were its most valuable asset at the end, not its product, which is a sign the product itself never achieved durable differentiation."]}
